stimulus:
Tomato ripening time at four storage temperatures, with and without exposure to a ripening agent.

TemperatureWith ripening agent (days)Without ripening agent (days)
10°C914
14°C711
18°C69
22°C56

A horticulture student studied how temperature and ripening-agent exposure affect tomato ripening. She treated one batch of tomatoes with a ripening agent and left another batch untreated, then ripened both batches at each of four temperatures. Comparing the data, the student concluded that ______
stem: Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to complete the student’s conclusion?

answerOptions.content: 10°C is the ideal temperature at which to store tomatoes to slow ripening time.
the ripening agent was associated with a greater absolute reduction in ripening time at 10°C, 14°C, and 18°C than at 22°C.
tomatoes treated with the ripening agent ripened faster at 10°C and 14°C than at 18°C and 22°C.
for tomatoes that were not treated, differences in temperature were not associated with differences in ripening time.

rationale: Choice B is the best answer because the table shows that the ripening agent reduced ripening time by 5 days at 10°C, 4 days at 14°C, 3 days at 18°C, but only 1 day at 22°C. The absolute reduction at the three cooler temperatures was larger than at 22°C. Choice A misuses the data — cooler tomatoes ripen most slowly with or without agent, but no data identify an ideal storage temperature. Choice C is contradicted by the data (tomatoes still ripen more slowly at cooler temperatures). Choice D is contradicted by the data: ripening time without agent ranges from 6 to 14 days across temperatures.
